text: The surgeon begins the procedure at the duodenum, performing a bottom-up lateral-to-medial dissection. The D-II segment and the head of the pancreas are carefully dissected. The right parietocolic gutter is opened using an ultrasonic device, extending the dissection up to the liver. The hepatic flexure is mobilized with the surgeon now positioned on the left side of the patient. The iliocolic and right branch of the middle colic vessels are ligated and sectioned. The mesocolon and mesentery are transected using the ultrasonic device.

The surgeon completes the right hemicolectomy by transecting the colon and terminal ileum using a sixty linear stapler, introduced through the right lower trochanter, with the surgeon positioned between the patient's legs.

The specimen is removed through a protected suprapubic incision. The pneumoperitoneum is restored, and the ileum and colon stumps are approximated in an antiperistaltic fashion without traction. The medial ends of both staple lines are excised, and a linear stapler is inserted through the anterotomies via the right lower trocar and fired. The surgeon is positioned between the legs, with the assistant on the left side of the patient. A stay suture is placed to ensure traction on the posterior and anterior walls of the anastomosis. The enterotomy is closed using a sixty blue cartridge from the left lower trocar.

The surgeon performs simultaneous anastomosis and resection by approximating the colon and ileum without division. Two enterotomies are created at the future anastomosis site, and the stapler is inserted and fired, maintaining the same surgeon position and trocar usage as previously described.

The surgeon closes the enterotomy transversely using a stapler, simultaneously resecting the proximal and distal margins. The surgical team ensures there is no mesenteric interposition posteriorly and carefully checks the length of the anastomosis.

The final result of the ileocolic resection is shown, with four crotch stitches securing the distal end of the anastomosis. The mesentery appears untwisted and without traction, indicating a successful completion of the procedure.
